title: Annual CO₂ emissions from deforestation for food production
subtitle: Tonnes of CO₂ emissions linked to deforestation for food production – it is trade-adjusted, to reflect the carbon footprint of diets within a given country. Based on the annual average over the period from 2010 to 2014.
note: Trade adjustment means that deforestation linked to the production of imported food products is added to a country's total; exported emissions are subtracted from its total.
caption:
Annual CO₂ emissions from deforestation for food production

Tonnes of CO₂ emissions linked to deforestation for food production – it is trade-adjusted, to reflect the carbon footprint of diets within a given country. Based on the annual average over the period from 2010 to 2014.

This choropleth world map displays annual CO₂ emissions from deforestation for food production, measured in tonnes and trade-adjusted to reflect the carbon footprint of diets within a given country based on the annual average from 2010 to 2014. The legend ranges from 0 to over 300 million tonnes, with specific thresholds at 3 million, 10 million, 30 million, 100 million, and 300 million tonnes. Brazil sits alone in the darkest red band, indicating emissions exceeding 300 million tonnes. Indonesia and the United States fall into the next darkest band, representing values between 100 million and 300 million tonnes. China, India, and Mexico appear in a mid-range orange shade corresponding to the 30 million to 100 million tonne bracket. Canada, Australia, Russia, and several European nations sit in lighter orange shades that align with the 3 million to 30 million tonne range. A large portion of the map, including most of Africa, the Middle East, and Central Asia, is covered in diagonal hatching to indicate no data. The trade adjustment methodology means that deforestation linked to the production of imported food products is added to a country's total, while exported emissions are subtracted from its total. The visual distribution highlights a concentration of high emissions in specific large economies and tropical regions, while vast areas of the globe lack reported figures for this specific metric.
